title: Strong demand for clean air policies in Majority World cities
core_conclusion: In a pre-registered survey of 11,417 adults across four severely polluted Majority-World cities (Accra, Delhi, Jakarta, Johannesburg), air quality was a highly prioritized concern and majorities supported all six mitigation policies, including cost-bearing ones. Concern, institutional trust and low perceived behavioral control were the strongest correlates of support, challenging the view of weak environmental demand in highly exposed developing settings.
why_it_matters: Overturns the 'limited environmentalism in poorer economies' assumption with direct policy-support evidence; fills a scarce cross-national measurement gap in the Majority World; pinpoints trust, concern and agency as actionable levers; centers the most exposed populations.
why_nature: Comparative four-city design yields a transferable claim about environmental demand; couples human attitudes to the urban environment and exposure context; strongly policy-translatable; pre-registered and fully reproducible (OSF + Zenodo).
added_value_to_my_research: Join survey demand to neighborhood PM2.5 surfaces and map perception-exposure gaps with MGWR
Replace LMG decomposition with explainable ML (SHAP) to separate compositional vs contextual drivers
Fuse demand + exposure + service access into a clean-air/clinic prioritization index (efficiency-equity)
Embed randomized framing experiments and model spatial heterogeneous treatment effects via ABM
Build a transferable GeoAI model predicting city-level support for unsurveyed megacities (FAIR/foundation-model)
